Case title: Doctoral-student AI use and training needs across dissertation workflows
Short description: Use doctoral-student AI adoption patterns to identify where training shifts from prompt fluency to provenance, validation, and calibration are most needed.
Empirical unit of analysis: Survey and interview data on doctoral students' AI-tool use
Candidate roles tested: Protocol and provenance architect; accountability and labor allocator
Key typology question: Are new researcher roles teachable and institutionally scaffolded, or only emergent in high-end labs?
Observable artifacts / traces: Survey responses, training logs, self-reported use by phase, perceived benefits/risks
Key outcomes observed: Phase-specific adoption, perceived skills gaps, governance expectations
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Prompt-centric training performs as well as provenance- and validation-centric training expectations
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Self-reports may not match actual workflow traces
Why this case has high leverage: Without a pedagogy case, the typology remains descriptive and misses role reproduction.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
